data.title: Hess Midstream: Solid Fundamentals And Low Valuation May Fuel Upside
data.description:
Three and a half months have passed since my previous coverage of Hess Midstream LP (HESM). If you remember, I still advise you to be a bit cautious about rushing into it. Yet, I also noted that valuation has already priced in the downside risks I explained in the very first article. This prompted me to give it a soft buy upgrade. True enough, the stock price trend improved and even gave 7% returns for a relatively short period. I still observe similar challenges it may face this year. However, I believe that HESM is still well-positioned with its strategic operations and robust fundamentals. Valuation is still reasonable and shows some upside potential. Technicals adhere to it as the uptrend holds and strengthens.

Hess Midstream LP: How It Has Been Lately

Hess Midstream LP is surrounded by skepticism due to its high dependence on Hess Corporation, now part of Chevron Corporation (CVX). Meanwhile, the oil and gas markets remain volatile due to various factors affecting their prices. Even so, HESM keeps proving many skeptics wrong as it continues to give solid results while looking for more ways to fortify its business operations. We have seen this in its recent performances.

For instance, its Q3 2025 revenues reached $420.9M, up by 11.2% YoY from $378.5M and by 1.5% from $414.2 QoQ. This YoY revenue growth was also much stronger than in Q3 2024 at only 4.2%. If we look at it per segment, HESM showed a stronger performance across all segments YoY and QoQ. One aspect to consider is its increased gas throughput volumes. Note that the natural gas price continues to rebound from its 2023-2024 lows. Higher prices made production more profitable, which led to more midstream volumes. This remained the growth driver of HESM amid the volatility in the oil market.

In addition, it kept its costs and expenses manageable despite the increased operating capacity. This was impressive considering stubborn inflation in the US. With that, its operating margin improved to 61.5% versus 61.1% YoY.

Challenges

HESS faces the same challenges today. Its very limited geographical diversification can lead to growth constraints in the long run. I’ve already explained that production growth in the Bakken region has plateaued in recent years. This can tell us that productivity has already weakened. However, this is not an automatic indication of oil depletion in Bakken. After all, it still has over 34M barrels of oil, averaging at 2,012 barrels per well.

Its high dependence on Chevron can also be a problem. CVX may jettison HESM if it’s deemed unnecessary for its growth. Currently, the affiliate revenue comprises 96% of the total amount. But I think the possibility of its sale may depend on the productivity of Bakken. As long as the region remains productive, CVX will keep HESM. The risk lies in the value of HESM after the sale. This may impact not only the business itself but also the investors.

Oil prices may also remain a challenge. Although they are not directly correlated with midstream operations, there will still be some impact. Now, Brent and WTI are $67.96 and $62.74, respectively. They remain at sub-$70 levels despite the ongoing tension in Iran and in the nearby regions. I think it may still take some time for their prices to break above $70 since the recent OPEC oil hikes seem to have already priced in these events. They keep offsetting one another. So, what does it mean for HESM? Lower oil prices mean lower earnings for oil producers. So, throughput volumes and rates in midstream operations may be affected.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these challenges, I believe that HESM can still get through these.

One thing I like about Hess Midstream LP is its simple and straightforward business model. HESM uses a fee-based model backed by minimum volume commitments. These are often long-term contracts, which may range for about 10 years and above. So, this makes HESM revenues and flow more predictable. For 2026-2028, HESM has already released its MVCs. Note that MVCs are set at 80% of CVX’s nomination every year. Once set, these can only be increased and not reduced. So even if volumes dip, CVX and third-party customers will still pay the minimum fee.

This is also a high-margin business with controlled capital intensity. Note that HESM has huge fixed costs relative to variable costs. In fact, most of these are upfront capex, such as processing plants, compression, pipelines, and water handling. So once they are built, the increase in power and labor costs becomes manageable. This is why its operating margin remains relatively flat above 60%. If throughput volumes and operating capacity increased revenue growth can easily offset the increase in costs and expenses. Lastly, because a huge portion of its customer base is CVX, it doesn’t have to think about customer acquisition costs. So, it saves a lot of money instead of spending it on sales and marketing while competing on prices. And because its model is fee-based and long-term it can shield itself from spot rate risks.

Another important thing to consider is the potential revival of the Bakken oil boom. Last January 2025, it was found that the Three Forks reservoir in the Bakken shale could hold about 250M barrels of untapped crude. As of that date, there were about 360 horizontal wells drilled, producing 92M barrels of oil and 238B cu ft of natural gas. This is said to only represent 2% of the overall drilling activity and 2% of the total output in the region. So, I may also confirm my view that the Bakken is still very far from depletion. Growth may have peaked and/or plateaued, but production may remain robust.

What’s more interesting is how HESM tries to capitalize on its untapped growth potential. One is its increasing exposure to third-party customers. While this is still nothing compared to CVX, revenues from this segment are already more than twice the value in Q3 2024. It also increased by 71% from my previous coverage, showing its larger expansion in the most recent quarter.

Its increasing production of natural gas may also be its strong growth catalyst. The price of natural may have been on a roller coaster ride in the past two months. But its strength has remained evident. Before Q4 ended, it reached its two-year high of $5.4. So, it is highly possible that HESM had higher throughput volumes and revenues from natural gas. This may be sustained by the data center expansion, which may increase in demand in the coming years. Note that the global data center capacity demand is expected to increase by 3.5x in 2025-2030. In turn, the US electricity demand may surge to 606 TWh. This may lead to an influx of natural gas demand in the long run.

Lastly, HESM maintains robust fundamentals. Because it’s basically infrastructure, and not manufacturing, it controls its operations very well. Its high profitability also means high liquidity. Its operating cash flow of $738.2M for three quarters is enough to cover the combined amount of capex and cash distributions.

HESM complements it with prudent debt management to maintain timely and appropriate borrowing repayments and additions. Its maturity profile is also decent since 99% of the total amount will not mature within a year. To be more specific, $1.150B has a maturity of about five years. This is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 3.1x, which means that it only needs 3.1 years to repay the whole amount at once even if it doesn’t have to. This proves that HESM still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operations and cash distributions.

Valuation

After updating its P/E Ratio, HESM still appears cheap today. Currently, it trades at only 12.68x its EPS versus the five-year average of 14.10x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP or target price, will be $39.62. I know that this is not conclusive since P/E only shows how cheap or expensive HESM is relative to previous years. It also does not reveal its intrinsic value. But this is enough to prove that HESM is trading at a cheap multiple. In fact, this is the cheapest level since 2020.

The market may not care much about this detail. What’s even more important is that the EPS should keep increasing. Most likely, the stock price will follow. And as you can see, the price has increased continuously in line with the increasing EPS. The trend since 2020 justifies the increasing price. ‘

To prove this, I used the regression analysis. I used the quarterly values of the EPS and applied the first-order differencing to limit the impact of seasonality. With that, I derived a correlation of 99.6%. This means that there’s a 99.6% probability that the price will follow the EPS trend. In addition, the P-value is very low at 0.000293, which means that only 0.003% of their relationship happened by chance.

To have a more forward-looking analysis, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is $3.02. The dividend growth rate is also the 2021-2025 CAGR. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 10.0%, which is twice the standard of 5.0%, to be very conservative. Lastly, I set its Beta at 1.00 to price it in line with the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, the TP is $44.54. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $40.09, which is very close to the P/E TP. To be honest, I believe that the stock is reasonably priced with or without the model. After all, the stock boasts a yield of 8.48%, which is already high.

Projection

A few days before the Q4 2025 release, one may be wondering about its performance for the whole year. With that, I decided to update my projection. To be conservative, I just set its 2025 revenue growth at 5% to derive $1.571B. It may appear low since it is much lower than the average growth rate of 10.5% in three quarters of 2025. So, I also make a higher estimation of 10% to $1.646B. These will be my revenue high and low. Meanwhile, I set its COGS and operating expenses as a percentage of revenue. Lastly, depreciation and capex remain the same since I derive them using the straight-line method. With that, I was able to derive NOPAT and EBITDA. In my view, my estimation makes sense and is even conservative if we relate it to its quarterly performance. Also, natural gas prices rose sharply, which could have led to higher throughput volumes. With higher sales and margins, I believe that HESM may sustain the uptrend.

Technicals

HESM’s technicals are stronger than in my previous coverage, although there’s still a possibility of a consolidation. But overall, its momentum has improved. The price is trending above the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum has already exceeded the average in the past 50 days. This is still lower than the 200-day SMA since it has not yet recovered from the selloff last Q3 2025. In addition, the 50-day SMA is starting to shift its direction upwards, which shows that market sentiments are becoming more optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show a stronger influence of buyers but still warrant some caution. The MACD line is moving above the signal line, which should not be surprising considering the increasing buying volume. The histogram is within positive territory, which implies that buying volume has already exceeded selling volume. Lastly, RSI shows that momentum is still upwards but advises us to be a bit more careful as the stock is starting to become overbought.

Conclusion

Hess Midstream LP remains resilient despite volatile market conditions. Its strategic exposure to natural gas and robust fundamentals ensure its sustainability and dividend longevity. Valuation is also still low today and shows some upside. Technicals adhere to these aspects as the uptrend continues despite the likelihood of overbuying. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Texas Instruments Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
I owned Texas Instruments (TXN) for almost 18 months and then managed to sell what appears to be a local bottom in hindsight. Although I did re-allocate my proceeds from TXN sale to Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta (META) equally, Texas Instruments performed better than either of those companies. The chart below doesn’t even include the earnings pop of TXN; it went up ~30% since I sold in November. As you can see, markets are a humbling machine!

Let me dig into 4Q’25 earnings of Texas Instruments (TI).

The big reveal in this call was TI’s revenue from data center which includes sectors related to data center compute, data center networking, rack power, and thermal management. TI’s website has a good interactive section to help you understand their offerings in data center.

Back in September, TI’s CEO mentioned that data center can eventually be ~20% of their business, which implied ~$4-5 billion revenue opportunity for them. They ended Q4 with $450 million run rate which was ~10% of their 4Q’25 revenue. Data center has been growing for 7 consecutive quarters, and looking at their estimate of eventual size of data center business, it is likely to continue to grow in the coming years. Management also clarified they are not chasing a single “socket”. Instead, they are targeting the “thousands of different parts” in a server rack, including power management, thermal sensing, and signal chain components.

Beyond data centers, all the major segments were down sequentially: industrials down MSD sequentially, automotive down LSD sequentially, and personal electronics down mid-teens sequentially.

Management reiterated that while they believe the recovery has legs in industrials, their visibility is limited beyond a quarter. From the call:

“…even if I go back to Q3, which was, I think, the highest industrial quarter for 2025, it was still about 25% from the previous peaks in year 2022, right? So I do believe that the secular growth continues in industrial. We are looking at end equipment and generation to generation, we see just more content growth per system. So I expect industrial to establish new highs in the future. This is why I talked in the last quarter about maybe a more moderate recovery, especially on the industrial side. I do want to remind us all that earlier in 2025, I would say, the first half of ‘25, we saw a pickup of industrial and then it kind of came down. We want to see how sustainable this wake up in orders is.”

In terms of margin, gross margin was down both QoQ and YoY; however, operating margin improved by ~86 bps YoY in 4Q’25. As you can see below, operating margins of mid-30s in 4Q’25 are still closer to the trough margins of this cycle last year, and still materially lower than the peak margins of ~50% in the 2021-22 period. Even if you consider the average of peak and trough as more “normalized” margins, there should be decent margin expansion over the course of the cyclical recovery.

While TI’s guide for Q1 tends to go down sequentially due to seasonal factors, they guided for $4.32 billion to $4.68 billion in revenue in 1Q’26. If they meet the high end of the guide, TI’s revenue will grow at +5.8% QoQ. More importantly, while some analysts speculated whether such unusual QoQ growth was pricing related, TI dispelled it during the call. From the call:

“we have 80,000 products. Prices always go up and down. But for the company, the overall price effect like-for-like in ‘25, we expected it to be low single digits down. Now we finished ‘25, it was exactly there. When you say low single digits, think about 2% or 3% down. That’s my assumption for 2026. That’s what we expect the market conditions to be. If anything changes with pricing, as you know, we’ll see -- of course, TI will respond. But right now, that’s our assumption moving forward. That’s why I was so convinced that the Q1, I think we have a little sequential growth there. It’s not due to pricing. Actually, usually, Q1 pricing usually goes down a little bit because of yearly negotiations. That’s usually what we see in Q1.we are just seeing growing orders, and it behaved the same through the quarter. I can’t speculate on what, but I do know the industrial market, there needs to be a correction. And the second point is data center is now a bigger part of our business, so it starts to move the numbers for us, right? This is a market that is now growing every quarter, and it’s not insignificant. So I think that also helps to change the guide compared to previous years.”

While TI received some flak for building too much inventory in the last couple of years, this cyclical recovery can now turn into a boon for them, especially if the pace of recovery surprises their competitors. So far, that’s not been the case, given the weak recovery. From the call:

Our lead times are very competitive, unchanged, I think, on average, below 13 weeks, many of our parts at 6 weeks. Part of our ambition and objective as we prepare to the next cycle, was to be -- to be able to maintain very competitive lead times across the cycle. So far this cycle has not been very tough to meet, right, as I said, been a slow recovery, but our lead times continue to stay very, very competitive, probably the lowest in the industry, and our inventory position allows us to support customers.

Overall, the tone in the call was quite positive even though I don’t think a whole lot has changed in the last three months. The stock currently trades at ~31x NTM EBIT. Given the cyclical recovery and margin expansion opportunity, I can see why the stock would trade at optically elevated multiples. Nonetheless, even if you adjust for normalized margins, the stock trades around mid-20s NTM EBIT multiple, which is fine, but not quite exciting.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AerCap: 2026 Outlook And Estimates. Is There Still Upside Left? Yes!
data.description:
AerCap (AER) capped off a strong 2025, rising 51% and crushing the S&P500.

I was very bullish on them entering 2025, and they have outperformed my high expectations. I originally had them at $10/share in core EPS with another $4/share in sales gains, and it looks like core EPS will be closer to $11.50 with $4-4.50 in sales gains.

Insurance recoveries were a big catalyst in 2025, collecting nearly $1.5 billion, but this is largely concluded now. On a price/book value basis, AerCap is nowhere near as cheap, trading at a 24% premium to my $115/share YE25 book value estimate.

Normally, this is where I'd advocate being more defensive, whether that means trimming the position or selling calls. But in AerCap's case, I think another 20-30% up in the year 2026 is possible. Let's walk through the key value drivers.

FY26 Core Earnings

This slide from their Q3 Earnings Presentation really illustrates the strength here, with core earnings rising from an initial $9 (midpoint) to likely $11.50, with another $4.50 of gain on sale. I see no reason why this growth won't continue between net interest margin continuing to improve while gain of sale and share repurchases continue.

From their latest 6-K, we see the new assets driving basic lease rents higher year over year, while the drag from older, COVID-era leases continues to diminish. Worth noting that this is the first 6-K I've seen where power-by-the-hour, or PBH, rents were not called out as the headwind; in fact, they aren't mentioned at all.

With the cash infusion from ongoing operations and insurance collections, interest expense was up for the year, but down 6% year over year in Q3. FY26 interest expense likely comes in under FY25.

Leverage and Net Interest Margin

Leverage always seems to be the most worrisome thing for many investors, and AerCap is leveraged. This isn't a negative per se, but it's notable that AerCap's leverage has decreased significantly in recent quarters.

AerCap's stated target debt-to-equity ratio is 2.5x, but they are running far lower

Gross debt has decreased while operating cash flow continues to grow at a healthy clip.

Higher lease rents and lower interest expenses are finally driving a higher net spread. Considering they are issuing debt with a narrow margin over treasuries (they recently issued 3-year notes at 4.125% and 7-year notes at 4.75%), interest expense looks likely to flatline here.

Repurchases

AerCap's Capital Recycling strategy is as straightforward as it is accretive. Sell assets for more than they're carried on the balance sheet; use that cash to repurchase shares. This was an absolute no brainer when AerCap shares traded below book value. Now, it's a bit more complex.

But I believe they're still a "no brainer" even 25% above book value because they're selling assets for more than 2x the carry cost. Updating an example I used in early 2025,

Selling at a 27% gain, repurchasing at book = 0.27 * (1+2.4) / 1.0 = 1.92x

Updating this for Q3, and repurchasing shares 24% above book

Selling at a 28% gain (Q3 reported GoS), repurchasing at 1.24x = 0.28 * (1+2.1) / 1.24 = 1.7x

Not as much firepower, but still very accretive. Worth noting, this would be 1.78x if the debt to equity was similar to 2024.

Is there a point at which these don't provide the appropriate margin of safety and the cash is better spent on something else? Yes, but I think we're still a good ways from this. I think repurchases are still worth it down to ~1.4x.

The buyback machine should still be in full force.

Conclusion

I thought there was plenty of upside left in September, and I still think there is. The catalysts I see are:

  • Continued net interest margin growth and repurchases.
  • Expiration of more COVID era leases and delivery of new aircraft.
  • The closing of the Air Lease (AL) take-private. Air Lease has a $7.2 billion market cap and is going private in the first half of 2026. Current Air Lease owners have few choices beyond AerCap if they want to remain exposed to the sector (perhaps also Willis Lease Finance (WLFC), another recent idea I've written up.)
  • Additional recovery in the widebody market, which saw significant recovery during FY25.

Projecting ahead for FY26, on an equivalent share count basis, I believe AerCap could do $13 of "core" earnings plus $5 in gain of sale. I see them repurchasing $2 billion in shares at $150/share, reducing the share count another 8%. All in, this gets close to $20/share in EPS, a 7x earnings multiple. If shares remain at current levels, they "earn" their way back to just 6% above stated book in 1 year, and back to book value a few quarters after that. That seems like a pretty good setup for FY26.

AerCap still has significant upside at these levels.

data.title: Roivant Sciences: King Of The Vants Prepares To Drop Vantism In Search Of Greater Growth
data.description:
This is my third look at Roivant Sciences (ROIV). I rated it a "Hold" in 07/2025's "Roivant Sciences: Booming Pipeline Without Product Revenues Creates Valuation Riddle". Now six months on, its share price has bumped ~110%. In doing so it has rewarded shareholders who were able to solve its valuation riddle.

As its vant model proves cumbersome, Roivant embraces a traditional development and commercialization model.

On its website, accessed 01/23/2026, Roivant describes its vant structure as:

We have companies in the Roivant family spanning multiple therapeutic areas, modalities, and technologies

It goes on to list these companies as follows:

In its Q2/2025 10-Q, Roivant includes the following partial list of its subsidiary vants:

Of the six above-listed companies, only two, Immunovant (IMVT) and Arbutus (ABUS) are publicly traded. Immunovant, in which Roivant has a 50% fully diluted interest, has a market cap of >$5 billion. Arbutus, in which Roivant has a 19% fully diluted interest, has a market cap of <$0.80 billion. Arbutus is not a vant so much as a partner to Genevant under a 2018 collaboration to develop RNA therapeutics for genetic diseases.

Combined, Arbutus and Immunovant make up ~$2.6 billion of Roivant's market cap of ~$16 billion or ~16.25%. Other vants mentioned in the 10-Q excerpt above include VantAI, Psivant, and Datavant. In the 10-Q's succeeding rundown of expected near-term catalysts, only four companies are mentioned:

  1. Roivant — parent company listed for its ongoing program of expected mid/late-stage in-licensing announcements;
  2. Genevant — is listed for its role in the U.S. Moderna case which is set for trial in Q1/2026;
  3. Immunovant — is listed for sundry upcoming data releases for IMVT-1402 in various indications plus for Batoclimab in thyroid eye disease [TED]; and
  4. Priovant — is listed for expected H1 NDA filing of brepocitinib in dermatomyositis and two upcoming data releases for brepocitinibin late stage trials.

The bottom line is that Roivant's vant structure has morphed into a complex web of ~10 fiefdoms. These fiefdoms have proven adept at generating cash; however, at this point Roivant is reevaluating whether it is the best structure for its future development.

Slide 3 of its J.P. Morgan Presentation includes the following epochal announcement:

Roivant’s next decade will look materially different from its last: now simplified to a “traditional development and commercialization” company with a near term commercial launch

In response to a question during its J.P. Morgan conference as to Roivant's expected structure over the long haul, CEO Gline expressed frustration over the challenges of his industry. He lamented that it did not matter how thoughtful or creative you were or how sophisticated your business model; what matters is developing effective medicines that help patients.

He opined that the way a company organizes itself and determines what sits where mostly doesn't matter. He further noted:

So I think a lot of that has kind of fallen away from our story in a way that I feel proud of that we no longer have to get up here and talk about the portfolio of Vants and who's funded where and which says which CEO, that stuff doesn't really drive the story. We are much simpler than we have been in moments in our past. We have fewer programs. They are programs of higher value. And I think that's a function of maturity and a function of realizing that it's much better to do a few things well than a lot of things not as well. And I think we've just learned a lot of those lessons. It is -- we do not have enough time here to talk about all the things that haven't worked for us. It's a long list. (emphasis added)

Ouch, that's a painful admission. Clearly, Gline was having one of those glass half-empty moments that strike everyone from time to time. As I will discuss below this is a sign of a maturation for the business. While it has had its challenges, it has also had its successes.

Roche's 10/2023 deal generated $7.1 billion in return for a phase 3 ready molecule.

It generally counts as a good day at the office when a biotech CEO inks a $7.1 billion cash-out-front deal for a phase 3-ready antibody. Such was the case for Roivant when it sold its Televant subsidiary to Roche (RHHBY). At the time, Roivant owned 75% of Televant, with Pfizer (PFE) owning the other 25%.

Now ~2years on Gline being the thoughtful executive that he is, has both had second thoughts about the deal while relishing the financial independence that it provides. This financial independence is a big deal. The slide 6 excerpt from its J.P. Morgan presentation describes its benefit below:

Gline discusses the significance of this as follows:

...being funded through profitability is an incredible privilege in biotech. It is not a privilege that most companies have. And sometimes, I'll talk to an investor and the investor would be like, oh, your stock trades funny, like something is different about it. It reacts differently to data, it moves in different ways. And I think part of the answer to that is like everybody else is doing financing. Everybody else is raising money when they put out good data, and I think like that just like affects the patterns of institutional investing activity in a way that we don't have. And so I think we are a different beast from that perspective. And I think there's very few other development-stage biotech companies that have that.

Perhaps he is putting his finger on the titular riddle referenced in my previous article; an explanation for Roivant's superior market performance per the chart below:

Near term catalysts are catnip for bulls while bears note speculative overlay to Roivant's prospects.

J.P. Morgan Presentation slide 5 below sets out a tasty menu of 6 diverse near-term upcoming catalysts:

The first of these Roivant's planned early 2026 Brepocitinib DM NDA filings refers to its expected filing for Brepocitinib in treatment of dermatomyositis (DM). DM is a rare disease which affects different patients differently. CEO Gline pegged it as a blockbuster opportunity during his J.P. Morgan discussion, where he emphasized the following taken from quoted excerpts:

  1. dermatomyositis is a terrible disease. It's an orphan inflammatory disease... [affecting] probably 70,000 people in America, of whom 40,000 are in active therapy;
  2. the only approved medicines for dermatomyositis are either like steroids and immunosuppressants like methotrexate or high-dose prednisone, or IVIG is approved;

  3. investors who do doc calls with dermatomyositis physicians, hear enthusiasm for our drug because the docs are legitimately enthusiastic for a new treatment option, specifically for a combo of JAK1 and TYK2.;

  4. based on the incredible clinical data that the team generated back in September that we put out, I think all of that has carried a lot of enthusiasm...that enthusiasm will serve us well assuming we're successful with FDA;

  5. brepocitinib in dermatomyositis should be a multibillion-dollar drug.

'Sounds like exciting stuff; time will tell is this just CEO puffery or if DM will actually help to justify meaningful chunks of Roivant's ~$16 billion market cap. Gline is not pinning all his expectations on DM.

He closes his J.P. Morgan report responding to a question as to when and from which assets Roivant expects to achieve its goal of generating $15 billion in peak sales. He responds with the following expansive overview of Roivant's multifaceted opportunities:

...Brepocitinib and FcRn for us, 1402 both are at this point already in clinical development in many indications, several of which in each case can and should be multi-blockbuster indications. So I think it's easier to chart the path towards $5 billion, $10 plus billion sales for either of those drugs. Which, again, it feels ridiculous to be sitting here and talking for a drug that hasn't launched about a $10 billion market opportunity. But on the other hand, they're really exciting drugs, and it's what we're supposed to do in these settings. So I think they're going to be really big drugs.

...you got to take it one foot in front of the other, though, right? We're going to launch in DM first. I think DM has the potential on its own to be a multibillion-dollar blockbuster opportunity. Then we're going to launch an NIU. I think that on its own has a chance to be a multibillion dollar opportunity. If we're successful in Graves', that will be the next one after that, probably, and then we'll start talking about PH-ILD and some of these other markets thereafter. So I think it's really about -- and this is one of the things that I love most about our situation right now. It's really about being able to stack these things on top of each other and build something that accumulates over time that makes me excited.

What to expect from upcoming earnings report.

Roivant reported its fiscal Q2/2025 earnings on 11/09/2025; its fiscal Q3 earnings are expected pre-market on 02/06/2026. Roivant's executives have been working overtime to communicate with shareholders recently with its comprehensive:

  1. 12/12/2025 Analyst/Investor Day report and 192 slide show — during this investor's day Gline and various other members of Roivant's management team discussed Roivant's past, present and future plans;
  2. 01/12/2026 report and presentation during the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference as herein discussed.

Accordingly one should expect little zing in Roivant's upcoming earnings report. Roivant has exceptionally strong ratings across all areas tracked by Seeking Alpha as reflected by the graphic below:

This optimism is reflected in Roivant's share price, as shown by the chart above. I see no reason to expect market-moving news from Roivant's upcoming earnings report.

Conclusion

Roivant talks a big game. To be clear, I do not simply dismiss this talk. However, I think that it has to prove it that it can, in fact, operate effectively as a traditional development and commercialization company. To date, Roivant vant Dermavant's 2022 approval for and launch of Vtama, a cream in the treatment of plaque psoriasis, is Roivant's only one.

Once, Vtama was bruited as a potential blockbuster. Initial sales were disappointing. Roivant sold Dermavant to Organon (OGN) for $175 million up front and milestones of up to $1.2 billion. When it comes to approvals and launches, that is all there is.

I am attracted to the Roivant story. I agree with Gline that it has great potential. However, at this point, I continue to rate Roivant as a "Hold".
